metadata.title: Claude, When Not.
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 the one accent. Do not retint.
metadata.greeting_note: HAI persona, shortest-form rule. 'Halo' = Indonesian/German casual, one word. Season finale warmth.
metadata.thesis: Knowing when not to use it IS the AI skill.
metadata.note: ILLUSTRATE LAW reel: UI at B00 (cold open with RESULT lines — the honest not-list), B06 (verdict artifact = the when/not card), B07 (handoff), B08 (outro). Inner beats B01–B05 use distinct custom Remotion scenes on cream stage. PREDICT at B04. Season recap at B05 (HaiSeasonDeck). COLD OPEN, SPARK-LINE, HANDOFF, OUTRO laws in force. Season finale episode — wraps H1–H5 arc. House red line: the one-question test (B03) explicitly teaches the ethical path and names the line plainly without teaching evasion.

beats.narration_text: … this is Liam, in for Bear. When should you not use Claude? This video gives you the honest list — not a vague 'use good judgment' disclaimer, but a specific when/not card you can apply to any situation you're in.

props.command: when should I NOT use you?
output: the first draft of anything you're learning to draft
facts you won't verify, decisions you'd hide

beats.narration_text: Use it for leverage. Quizzing yourself on your own material — that's retrieval practice, the most evidence-backed study technique there is. Getting feedback on a draft you wrote yourself. Asking for explanations of concepts you're stuck on. These uses amplify your own work without replacing it.

props.heading: Use it for leverage.
props.body: Quizzing yourself on your own material — that's retrieval practice, the most evidence-backed study technique there is. Getting feedback on a draft you wrote yourself. Asking for explanations of concepts you're stuck on. These uses amplify your own work without replacing it.

beats.narration_text: Don't use it for: graded work without disclosure — that's the line the policy beat drew. Don't use it to generate practice problems you won't actually work through. And don't use it to skip the struggle on something where the struggle is the learning: the first draft of an essay, the first pass at a problem, the initial attempt at a skill.

props.heading: Don't use it for: graded work without disclosure — that's the line the policy be
props.body: Don't use it to generate practice problems you won't actually work through. And don't use it to skip the struggle on something where the struggle is the learning: the first draft of an essay, the first pass at a problem, the initial attempt at a skill.

beats.narration_text: The one question that collapses all the edge cases: would you hide this use from your teacher? If the answer is yes, it's a don't-use. If you'd be comfortable saying 'I used Claude to quiz myself on this material before the exam,' that's a legitimate use. The test isn't about what Claude can do — it's about what you'd disclose.

props.heading: The one question that collapses all the edge cases: would you hide this use from
props.body: If you'd be comfortable saying 'I used Claude to quiz myself on this material before the exam,' that's a legitimate use. The test isn't about what Claude can do — it's about what you'd disclose.

beats.narration_text: Commit before the reveal: which of these four is a don't-use? A: ask Claude to quiz you on chapter 4. B: ask Claude to write your essay. C: ask Claude to explain a concept you don't understand from lecture. D: ask Claude to suggest three examples you could use in your essay.

props.heading: Commit before the reveal: which of these four is a don't-use? A: ask Claude to q
props.body: B: ask Claude to write your essay. C: ask Claude to explain a concept you don't understand from lecture. D: ask Claude to suggest three examples you could use in your essay.

beats.narration_text: B. Writing your essay removes the drafting — which is the skill the assignment is developing. The other three are all legitimate uses: quizzing, explaining, and suggesting examples you then evaluate and use or reject are amplifiers of your work, not replacements for it.

props.heading: B.
props.body: Writing your essay removes the drafting — which is the skill the assignment is developing. The other three are all legitimate uses: quizzing, explaining, and suggesting examples you then evaluate and use or reject are amplifiers of your work, not replacements for it.

beats.narration_text: The when/not card. Use for: quizzing, feedback on your drafts, explanations, example suggestions, practice generation, research starting points. Don't use for: graded submissions without disclosure, skipping the productive struggle, replacing work you're accountable for.

artifactLines: USE for: quizzing yourself, feedback on YOUR draft, explanations of confusing concepts, practice generation, planning.
DON'T USE for: graded work without disclosure, first drafts you're supposed to learn by drafting, unverified facts, anything you'd hide.
The one question: would you hide this use from your teacher or boss? If yes — don't. Not fear of being caught. The hiding means the use is taking something that was supposed to be yours.
Knowing when not to use it IS the AI skill.

beats.narration_text: Your move. The handoff prompt has Claude print you a wallet-card version of the when/not rules — a short, paste-ready format you can keep near your workspace. Paste it in.

props.command: Give me a short wallet-card version of when I should and shouldn't use AI as a [student / mid-career professional]. Max 6 bullet points total. Practical and direct.

beats.narration_text: Claude, When Not. Season one, done. If the line is clear now, subscribe. HAI. Liam, in for Bear.
